title: Heading hierarchy

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific HTML: Semantic and Accessible Web Documents draft for Heading hierarchy; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: HTML Living Standard; living standard accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Heading hierarchy
sources.claim: Headings label document sections and form a meaningful hierarchy that supports scanning and assistive navigation.

sources.locator: Web Content Accessibility Guidelines 2.2; W3C Recommendation 2023; provisions applicable to Heading hierarchy
sources.claim: Headings label document sections and form a meaningful hierarchy that supports scanning and assistive navigation.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Headings label document sections and form a meaningful hierarchy that supports scanning and assistive navigation.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A page uses one descriptive h1 for its main subject and h2 elements for peer sections such as syllabus and schedule. Your goal is to create and review a logical heading hierarchy for a multi-section page. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Standards-mode document skeleton. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: Headings label document sections and form a meaningful hierarchy that supports scanning and assistive navigation. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Heading rank should reflect structure rather than visual size, and skipping ranks can obscure relationships even when browsers render them. This draft therefore rejects: Heading elements should be chosen by the font size the design needs.

includes: Use descriptive headings to communicate document outline rather than visual size.
Create and review a logical heading hierarchy for a multi-section page.
excludes: Heading rank should reflect structure rather than visual size, and skipping ranks can obscure relationships even when browsers render them.

outcomes: Create and review a logical heading hierarchy for a multi-section page.

examples.title: Heading hierarchy: concrete case
examples.situation: A page uses one descriptive h1 for its main subject and h2 elements for peer sections such as syllabus and schedule.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A page uses one descriptive h1 for its main subject and h2 elements for peer sections such as syllabus and schedule.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Choose heading rank from section hierarchy, then control visual size with CSS rather than selecting headings by appearance.
examples.result: The result follows because Choose heading rank from section hierarchy, then control visual size with CSS rather than selecting headings by appearance.

examples.title: Heading hierarchy: boundary case
examples.situation: Heading rank should reflect structure rather than visual size, and skipping ranks can obscure relationships even when browsers render them.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Start from the documented boundary: Heading rank should reflect structure rather than visual size, and skipping ranks can obscure relationships even when browsers render them.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: Heading elements should be chosen by the font size the design needs.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: Choose heading rank from section hierarchy, then control visual size with CSS rather than selecting headings by appearance.

analogy.example: A labeled document outline can model Heading hierarchy, with structure carrying meaning before decoration.

limitations: Documents cannot model parser recovery, browser APIs, or interactive semantics exactly.
